prompt_template:
Translate this Sanskrit verse from Vasubandhu's Triṃśikā into English. Give only the translation.

{verse}

verses.1: Indeed, the manifold conventional designation of self and dharmas that arises occurs in the transformation of consciousness; and that transformation is threefold.
verses.2: It is maturation, that called mentation, and the representation of objects; among these, the consciousness called the storehouse is maturation, containing all seeds.
verses.3: It has an unperceived representation of appropriation and place, and is always accompanied by contact, attention, feeling, perception, and volition.
verses.4: In it, feeling is neutral; it is unobscured and morally indeterminate, and so too are contact and the other mental factors. It flows on like a current.
verses.5: It ceases in the state of arhatship. Depending on it and taking it as its object, there arises the consciousness called manas, whose nature is mentation.
verses.6: It is always accompanied by four afflictions, obscured and indeterminate: self-view, self-delusion, self-conceit, and self-love.
verses.7: Arising on whatever plane, it is accompanied by factors belonging to that plane; it is not present in the arhat, nor in the attainment of cessation, nor on the supramundane path.
verses.8: This is the second transformation. The third is the apprehension of the sixfold object; it is wholesome, unwholesome, or neither.
verses.9: It is associated with the omnipresent, the determinate, and the wholesome mental factors, as well as with the afflictions and secondary afflictions, and it has the three kinds of feeling.
verses.10: The first are contact and the rest; desire, resolve, and mindfulness, together with concentration and discernment, are the specific ones; then come faith, shame, and moral scruple.
verses.11: Non-greed, non-hatred, and non-delusion, energy, pliancy, heedfulness, and non-harming are wholesome; the afflictions are attachment, aversion, and delusion.
verses.12: Conceit, wrong views, and doubt; concealment, spite, jealousy, then stinginess, together with deceit.
verses.13: Deceit, vanity, cruelty, shamelessness, lack of moral restraint, torpor, agitation, faithlessness, laziness, heedlessness, and forgetfulness.
verses.14: Distraction, lack of clear awareness, remorse, drowsiness, initial thought, and examination—these are secondary afflictions; the two pairs are twofold.
verses.15: The five consciousnesses arise in the root consciousness according to conditions, together or separately, like waves in water.
verses.16: Mental consciousness arises at all times, except among the non-percipient beings, in the two meditative attainments, in deep sleep, in fainting, and in other mindless states.
verses.17: This transformation of consciousness is conceptual construction; what is constructed by it does not exist. Therefore all this is mere representation.
verses.18: For consciousness contains all seeds; its transformation proceeds in various ways through mutual influence, and from this arises this or that conceptual construction.
verses.19: The latent impressions of karma, together with the impressions of the twofold grasping, generate another maturation when the previous maturation has been exhausted.
verses.20: Whatever thing is imagined by whatever conceptual construction is only the imagined nature; it does not exist.
verses.21: The dependent nature, however, is conceptual construction arising from conditions; the perfected nature is its perpetual absence of the former.
verses.22: For this very reason, it is to be said to be neither different from nor identical with the dependent nature, like impermanence and the like; when this is not seen, that is not seen.
verses.23: With reference to the threefold lack of intrinsic nature in the threefold nature, the lack of intrinsic nature of all phenomena was taught.
verses.24: The first is without intrinsic nature by its very characteristic; the other, again, because it does not arise by itself—this is another absence of intrinsic nature.
verses.25: It is the ultimate reality of all phenomena, and therefore it is also suchness; because it is such at all times, it is itself mere cognition.
verses.26: As long as consciousness does not abide in mere representation, the latent tendency toward the twofold grasping does not cease.
verses.27: Even while holding, “This is mere representation only,” one does not abide in that mere representation, for one still apprehends something set before oneself.
verses.28: But when cognition no longer apprehends an object, then it is established in mere representation, since, in the absence of what is grasped, there is no grasping of it.
verses.29: It is without mind, without apprehension; and that is supramundane knowledge—the transformation of the basis through the abandonment of the twofold affliction.
verses.30: That very state is the undefiled realm, inconceivable, wholesome, constant, and blissful; it is the body of liberation, called the Dharma-body of the Great Sage.
